Roman villa A Roman villa was a country house built for the upper class in the Roman Republic and the Roman Empire, similar in form to the hacienda estates in the colonies of the Spanish Empire. Pliny the Elder (23-79CE) distinguished two kinds of villas: the "villa urbana", a country seat that could easily be reached from Rome (or another city) for a night or two; and the "villa rustica", the farm-house estate permanently occupied by the servants who generally had charge of the estate. Battle of the Teutoburg Forest The Battle of the Teutoburg Forest (, "Hermannsschlacht", or "Varusschlacht", ), described as the Varian Disaster ("Clades Variana") by Roman historians, took place in the Teutoburg Forest in 9 CE, when an alliance of Germanic tribes ambushed and decisively destroyed three Roman legions and their auxiliaries, led by Publius Quinctilius Varus. The alliance was led by Arminius, a Germanic officer of Varus' auxilia. Arminius had acquired Roman citizenship and had received a Roman military education, which enabled him to deceive the Roman commander methodically and anticipate the Roman army's tactical responses. Battle of Lewes The Battle of Lewes was one of two main battles of the conflict known as the Second Barons' War. It took place at Lewes in Sussex, on 14 May 1264. It marked the high point of the career of Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester, and made him the "uncrowned King of England". Henry III left the safety of Lewes Castle and St. Pancras Priory to engage the Barons in battle and was initially successful, his son Prince Edward routing part of the baronial army with a cavalry charge. The victory of the Germanic tribes against Rome's legions in the Teutoburg Forest would have far-reaching effects on the subsequent history of both the ancient Germanic peoples and the Roman Empire. Contemporary and modern historians have generally regarded Arminius' victory over Varus as "Rome's greatest defeat", one of the most decisive battles recorded in military history, and as "a turning-point in world history". In 4 CE the Roman general (and later emperor) Tiberius entered Germania battlefield and left Henry's men exposed. Henry was forced to launch an infantry attack up Offham Hill where he was defeated by the barons' men, defending the hilltop. The royalists fled back to the castle and priory and the King was forced to sign the Mise of Lewes, ceding many of his powers to Montfort. Martius also speculated that the fish were attracted by the "odor" of urine. Later experimental evidence has shown this to be false, as the fish actually hunt by sight and have no attraction to urine at all. Another report from French naturalist Francis de Castelnau in 1855 relates an allegation by local Araguay fisherman, saying that it is dangerous to urinate in the river as the fish "springs out of the water and penetrates into the urethra by ascending the length of the liquid column." While Castelnau himself dismissed this claim as "absolutely preposterous," and the fluid mechanics of such a maneuver defy the laws of physics, it remains one of the

Longitude rewards The longitude rewards were the system of inducement prizes offered by the British government for a simple and practical method for the precise determination of a ship's longitude at sea. The rewards, established through an Act of Parliament (the Longitude Act) in 1714, were administered by the Board of Longitude. This was by no means the first reward to be offered to solve this problem. Philip II of Spain offered one in 1567, Philip III in 1598 offered 6,000 ducats and a pension, whilst the States General of the Netherlands offered 10,000 florins shortly after. In 1675 Robert

Candiru Candiru ("Vandellia cirrhosa"), also known as cañero, toothpick fish, or vampire fish, is a species of parasitic freshwater catfish in the family Trichomycteridae native to the Amazon Basin where it is found in the countries of Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru. The definition of "candiru" differs between authors. The word has been used to refer to only "Vandellia cirrhosa", the entire genus "Vandellia", the subfamily Vandelliinae, or even the two subfamilies Vandelliinae and Stegophilinae. Although some candiru species have been known to grow to a size of in length, others are considerably smaller. These smaller species are known

Longitude Act only addressed the determination of longitude at sea. Determining longitude reasonably accurately on land was, from the 17th century onwards, possible using the Galilean moons of Jupiter as an astronomical 'clock'. The moons were easily observable on land, but numerous attempts to reliably observe them from the deck of a ship resulted in failure. For details on other efforts towards determining the longitude, see History of longitude. The need for better navigational accuracy for increasingly longer oceanic voyages had been an issue explored by many European nations for centuries before the passing of the Longitude Act in England for an alleged tendency to invade and parasitise the human urethra; however, despite ethnological reports dating back to the late 19th century, the first documented case of the removal of a candiru from a human urethra did not occur until 1997, and even that incident has remained a matter of controversy. Candirus are small fish. Members of the genus "Vandellia" can reach up to in standard length, but some others can grow to around . They have a rather small head and a belly that can appear distended, especially after a large blood meal. The body is translucent, making it

Spassky in 1952. Karpov finished first in his first international tournament in Třinec several months later, ahead of Viktor Kupreichik. In 1967, he won the annual European Junior Championship at Groningen. Karpov won a gold medal for academic excellence in high school, and entered Moscow State University in 1968 to study mathematics. He later transferred to Leningrad State University, eventually graduating from there in economics. One reason for the transfer was to be closer to his coach, grandmaster Semyon Furman, who lived in Leningrad. In his writings, Karpov credits Furman as a major influence on his development as a world-class

Anatoly Karpov Anatoly Yevgenyevich Karpov (; born May 23, 1951) is a Russian chess grandmaster and former World Champion. He was the official world champion from 1975 to 1985 when he was defeated by Garry Kasparov. He played three matches against Kasparov for the title from 1986 to 1990, before becoming FIDE World Champion once again after Kasparov broke away from FIDE in 1993. He held the title until 1999, when he resigned his title in protest against FIDE's new world championship rules. For his decades-long standing among the world's elite, Karpov is considered by many to be one of

1784. Between 1774 and 1800, Spain sent several expeditions to Alaska in order to assert its claim over the Pacific Northwest. In 1789 a Spanish settlement and fort were built in Nootka Sound. These expeditions gave names to places such as Valdez, Bucareli Sound, and Cordova. Later, the Russian-American Company carried out an expanded colonization program during the early-to-mid-19th century. Sitka, renamed New Archangel from 1804 to 1867, on Baranof Island in the Alexander Archipelago in what is now Southeast Alaska, became the capital of Russian America. It remained the capital after the colony was transferred to the United States.

Abbotsford House Abbotsford is a historic country house in the Scottish Borders, near Melrose, on the south bank of the River Tweed. It was formerly the residence of historical novelist and poet, Sir Walter Scott. It is a Category A Listed Building. The nucleus of the estate was a small farm of , called Cartleyhole, nicknamed Clarty (i.e., muddy) Hole, and was bought by Scott on the lapse of his lease (1811) of the neighbouring house of Ashestiel. He first built a small villa and named it Abbotsford, creating the name from a ford nearby where previously abbots of Melrose

stage for the upcoming rivalries during the 14th and 15th centuries. This rivalry grew worse during and after the Protestant Reformation, when religion was another reason for conflict between the clans. The Gordon family adhered to Catholicism and the Forbes to Protestantism. Aberdeenshire was the historic seat of the clan Dempster. Three universities were founded in the area prior to the 17th century, King's College in Old Aberdeen (1494), Marischal College in Aberdeen (1593), and the University of Fraserburgh (1597). After the end of the Revolution of 1688, an extended peaceful period was interrupted only by such fleeting events such

body, both beneficial and harmful. Sodium compounds have been known since ancient times; salt (sodium chloride) has been an important commodity in human activities, as testified by the English word "salary", referring to "salarium", money paid to Roman soldiers for the purchase of salt. While potash has been used since ancient times, it was not understood for most of its history to be a fundamentally different substance from sodium mineral salts. As of late 2009, Audi's operating profit of €1.17-billion ($1.85-billion) made it the biggest contributor to parent Volkswagen Group's nine-month operating profit of €1.5-billion, while the other marques in Group such as Bentley and SEAT had suffered considerable losses. May 2011 saw record sales for Audi of America with the new Audi A7 and Audi A3 TDI Clean Diesel. In May 2012, Audi reported a 10% increase in its sales—from 408 units to 480 in the last year alone. Audi manufactures vehicles in seven plants around the

were handed down from generation to generation. Indirectly, fragments of this oral history have reached us via the literature of the Scandinavians and Germans, neighbors of the Huns who wrote between the 9th and 13th centuries. Attila is a major character in many Medieval epics, such as the Nibelungenlied, as well as various Eddas and sagas. Archaeological investigation has uncovered some details about the lifestyle, art, and warfare of the Huns. There are a few traces of battles and sieges, but today the tomb of Attila and the location of his capital have not yet been found. The Huns were

of a number of new works on classification. Extending the system developed by Sclater for birds—which divided the earth into six separate geographic regions for describing species distribution—to cover mammals, reptiles and insects as well, Wallace created the basis for the zoogeographic regions still in use today. He discussed all of the factors then known to influence the current and past geographic distribution of animals within each geographical region. These included the effects of the appearance and disappearance of land bridges (such as the one currently connecting North America and South America) and the effects of periods of increased glaciation.

for here. A 14th-century Mamluk mosque stands here with its white dome visible from most areas in and around Petra. Although his father is described as both an apostle and a prophet, Aaron is merely described as a prophet. The Kitab-I-Iqan describes Imran as being his father. Aaron appears paired with Moses frequently in Jewish and Christian art, especially in the illustrations of manuscript and printed Bibles. He can usually be distinguished by his priestly vestments, especially his turban or miter and jeweled breastplate. He frequently holds a censor or, sometimes, his flowering rod. In November 2014, Apple arranged for all App Store revenue in a two-week period to go to the fundraiser, generating more than US$20 million, and in March 2017, it released an iPhone 7 with a red color finish. Apple contributes financially to fundraisers in times of natural disasters. Ginsberg took part in decades of non-violent political protest against everything from the Vietnam War to the War on Drugs. His poem "September on Jessore Road", calling attention to the plight of Bangladeshi refugees, exemplifies what the literary critic Helen Vendler described as Ginsberg's tireless persistence in protesting against "imperial politics, and persecution of the powerless." His collection "The Fall of America" shared the annual U.S. National Book Award for Poetry in 1974. In 1979 he received the National Arts Club gold medal and was inducted into the American Academy and Institute of Arts and Letters. Ginsberg was a Pulitzer

Arts Foundation promotes and markets Native art from all regions and cultures in the State, using the internet. Influences on music in Alaska include the traditional music of Alaska Natives as well as folk music brought by later immigrants from Russia and Europe. Prominent musicians from Alaska include singer Jewel, traditional Aleut flautist Mary Youngblood, folk singer-songwriter Libby Roderick, Christian music singer-songwriter Lincoln Brewster, metal/post hardcore band 36 Crazyfists and the groups Pamyua and Portugal. The Man. There are many established music festivals in Alaska, including the Alaska Folk Festival, the Fairbanks Summer Arts Festival, the Anchorage Folk Festival, the

to natural market processes, or by perpetual provision of superior quality products and services. Furthermore, as demonstrated by the medieval systems in Ireland and Iceland, treating the right to justice as a property means that it is sold (not purchased) by victims. Some libertarians propose a restitution system of justice in which the right to restitution created by the violation of the victims' property could be homesteaded by bounty hunters that would bring criminals to justice,

Audi will also be the first manufacturer to use a 3D LIDAR system in addition to cameras and ultrasonic sensors for their AI. surgical grafts, and this led to his Nobel Prize in 1912. During World War I (1914–1918), Carrel and the English chemist Henry Drysdale Dakin developed the Carrel–Dakin method of treating wounds based on chlorine (Dakin's solution) which, preceding the development of antibiotics, was a major medical advance in the care of traumatic wounds. For this, Carrel was awarded the Légion d'honneur. Carrel co-authored a book with famed pilot Charles A. Lindbergh, "The Culture of Organs", and worked with Lindbergh in the mid-1930s to create the "perfusion pump," which allowed living organs to exist outside the body during surgery. The advance

explain the theological and cultural transitions which took place between the Old and New Testaments. They are also sometimes called "intertestamental" by religious groups who do not recognize Hellenistic Judaism as belonging with either "Jewish" or "Christian" testaments. Slightly varying collections of apocryphal, deuterocanonical, or intertestamental books of the Bible form part of the Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox and Oriental Orthodox canons (cf Development of the Old Testament canon). The deuterocanonical or intertestamental books of the Catholic Church include 1-2 Esdras, Tobit, Judith, Additions to Esther, the Wisdom of Solomon, Ecclesiasticus, Baruch, the Letter of Jeremiah, the Prayer of Azariah,

songs on the Project's albums are credited to "Woolfson/Parsons". Alan Parsons met Eric Woolfson in the canteen of Abbey Road Studios in the summer of 1974. Parsons had already acted as Assistant Engineer on the Beatles' albums "Abbey Road" (1969) and "Let It Be" (1970), had recently engineered Pink Floyd's "The Dark Side of the Moon" (1973), and had produced several acts for EMI Records. Woolfson, a songwriter and composer, was working as a session pianist; he had also composed material for a concept album idea based on the work of Edgar Allan Poe. was traditionally high in Armenia and topped all other republics of USSR and most of countries in Eastern Europe in 1978-1980. In The Sound and the Fury, memory operates as a fragmented and unreliable force that shapes the lives of the Compson family. Through Quentin, Faulkner presents a version of memory that is both obsessive and destructive. His recollections of his sister Caddy and their family’s decline are distorted by time, guilt, and his inability to move forward. The novel emphasizes how the past, in all its painful complexity, continually invades the present, preventing the characters from finding peace or redemption. Faulkner’s portrayal of memory shows how personal history can distort reality and interfere with one’s ability to live in the present.

Benjy’s sensory perception and his inability to grasp time contribute heavily to the fragmented narrative structure of The Sound and the Fury. His memories are detached from linear progression, blurring the line between past and present. As a result, his recollections serve as emotional markers rather than precise accounts of events.

In Lord of the Flies, survival is portrayed as a mental and moral struggle, not merely a physical one. As the boys’ sense of civilization deteriorates, they confront their inner instincts and capacity for cruelty. The battle for survival is often internal, as characters like Ralph and Piggy struggle to maintain order and morality against the primal forces embodied by Jack’s group. The novel suggests that true survival is not just about enduring the environment but also about holding on to human decency and civilization amidst chaos.

The boys’ struggle for food and shelter is one of the most obvious aspects of survival in Lord of the Flies. Their basic needs become increasingly difficult to meet as their social order unravels. The novel illustrates how the breakdown of society leads to a basic fight for survival that goes beyond physical needs, touching on the need for community and leadership.

In The Odyssey, fate is deeply entwined with the theme of perseverance. Odysseus’s long journey home is shaped by his ability to make choices despite the circumstances, illustrating that while fate may set the stage for certain events, human agency and willpower still play a significant role. His intelligence, cunning, and bravery are often the key factors that determine his success or failure, suggesting that fate is not an immutable force but something that can be confronted and overcome through effort and determination. The novel portrays fate as a dynamic force that requires active engagement to navigate.

The divine interventions of gods and goddesses throughout The Odyssey shape much of the narrative. These divine forces often guide or hinder Odysseus’s journey, emphasizing the belief that fate is largely determined by the whims of higher powers, beyond the control of mortals.

In The Brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky explores justice through the moral struggles of his characters, particularly in the context of the criminal justice system and the nature of sin. Dmitri’s trial serves as a critical moment in the novel, questioning whether true justice is ever possible in a world where human beings are driven by passion, pride, and suffering. The narrative presents a moral and philosophical inquiry into whether humans can ever be truly just, or if justice itself is a construct that fails to account for the complexities of human nature. The characters' actions and decisions reveal that justice is often intertwined with personal redemption and the quest for forgiveness.

Father Zosima’s teachings in The Brothers Karamazov offer a counterpoint to the darker aspects of justice. His belief in spiritual redemption and the power of forgiveness stands in contrast to the punitive nature of the justice system explored throughout the novel. Zosima's philosophy emphasizes mercy over judgment, pointing to a more transcendent view of justice.

Redemption in Crime and Punishment is intertwined with the characters’ struggles to reconcile their actions with their morality. Sonia, who maintains her faith and compassion despite her own suffering, becomes a symbol of spiritual redemption. Through her, Raskolnikov begins to understand that true redemption lies not in intellectual reasoning but in humility, compassion, and love. The novel suggests that redemption is not an instantaneous event but a gradual, painful process that requires a deep confrontation with one’s own guilt and suffering.

Raskolnikov’s confession at the end of Crime and Punishment marks his moment of redemption. His admission of guilt and his willingness to accept the consequences of his actions signal his moral awakening. This final act of repentance allows him to begin his journey toward spiritual renewal, as he acknowledges the weight of his crime.

In Macbeth, ambition is portrayed as a corrupting and all-consuming force that impacts not only those who seek power but also those who are drawn into their orbit. Lady Macbeth’s ambition pushes her to manipulate her husband, showing how the thirst for power can manifest through manipulation and coercion. Her ambition becomes so overwhelming that it drives her to madness, illustrating the psychological toll that unchecked ambition can take on an individual. The play explores how ambition can warp relationships, leading characters to betray their own values and succumb to moral degradation.

Macbeth’s desire for the throne is the central driving force behind his actions. His ambition leads him to murder King Duncan, setting off a chain of events that results in his own downfall. His relentless pursuit of power ultimately isolates him and leads to his psychological unraveling.

In The Great Gatsby, time is portrayed as an elusive and distorted force, particularly through the lens of the characters’ desires and the American Dream. The novel presents the 1920s as a period of excess and reinvention, where characters are constantly attempting to shape or escape their personal histories. Nick Carraway’s reflections on time are often imbued with a sense of longing and nostalgia, as he looks back on the events that transpired with a sense of inevitability. The novel ultimately explores how time can’t be controlled, and how the past, no matter how deeply one wishes to revise it, is always present in shaping the future.

Gatsby’s past plays a critical role in The Great Gatsby, as he attempts to rewrite it in order to recapture his lost love. His belief in the possibility of recreating the past leads to his tragic demise. Gatsby’s pursuit of Daisy is tied to his vision of a perfect future, which is, in part, defined by the illusion of time being malleable.

In Animal Farm, leadership is shown to be a reflection of power dynamics, where the individuals who assume authority often exploit their positions for personal gain. Snowball, in contrast to Napoleon, initially attempts to lead with ideals of equality and progress, but his leadership is overshadowed by those who seek control and manipulation. The farm's gradual descent into tyranny exemplifies how leadership can be twisted to serve the interests of the few, even when it begins with the most noble of intentions. Orwell critiques the corrupting nature of power and the ways in which it can be used to manipulate the masses, rendering the ideals of the revolution meaningless.

Napoleon’s rise to power in Animal Farm signals the shift from a society based on equality to one driven by authoritarian control. Through manipulation, coercion, and the elimination of rivals, Napoleon consolidates his power and becomes a tyrant, reflecting Orwell’s critique of the corrupting influence of power.

In One Hundred Years of Solitude, freedom is depicted as both a dream and a curse. Characters such as Remedios the Beauty embody a type of innocence that is free from the constraints of the world around them, but even this kind of freedom comes with its own costs. The novel explores how societal expectations, personal desires, and historical forces converge to limit individual freedom. The recurring arrival of foreign powers and the cyclical nature of life on the town of Macondo show how freedom can be both an illusion and a fleeting reality, often contingent on forces outside the individual's control.

The Buendía family’s cyclical nature in One Hundred Years of Solitude illustrates how freedom is constrained by both history and the repetition of personal mistakes. Their inability to break free from the patterns of their ancestors reflects a fundamental lack of agency, as each generation is condemned to relive the past, unable to escape the fates imposed upon them.

In The Catcher in the Rye, morality is explored through Holden Caulfield’s personal struggle with the idea of innocence versus corruption. He is deeply troubled by the perceived loss of innocence in the world, particularly in the lives of children, and this affects his view of morality. Holden’s actions often contradict his desire to protect innocence, as he himself is constantly grappling with his own moral compass. The novel reflects the complexity of moral judgment, where personal ideals can conflict with the messy reality of growing up and facing the world’s imperfections. Holden's moral dilemmas underscore the tension between idealism and the inevitable realities of life.

Holden Caulfield’s critiques of adult society shape much of the novel’s moral framework, as he believes that adults are inherently phony and corrupt. His desire to protect the innocence of children from the perceived evils of adulthood becomes a central moral struggle, ultimately leading to his emotional breakdown.

In The Stranger, identity is portrayed as a construct that is influenced by society’s expectations and the individual’s personal choices. Meursault’s indifferent attitude toward life and death challenges conventional ideas of selfhood, as he refuses to conform to the typical social roles that others expect of him. His sense of identity is shaped by his actions, or lack thereof, as he refuses to feign emotion or express beliefs he doesn’t feel. The novel critiques the ways in which identity is often dictated by external pressures, suggesting that true authenticity may come from rejecting societal norms entirely.

Meursault’s emotional indifference plays a pivotal role in The Stranger, as his lack of emotional response to significant life events—such as his mother’s death and his own trial—marks him as an outsider in society. His failure to conform to expected emotional reactions alienates him from those around him and drives the plot’s exploration of existentialism and the absurd.

In Wuthering Heights, nature is intertwined with the emotional landscapes of the characters. The stormy weather and harsh elements often mirror the tumultuous emotions of Heathcliff and Catherine, acting as a reflection of their inner turmoil. The contrast between the stark, often oppressive landscape of Wuthering Heights and the more temperate setting of Thrushcross Grange emphasizes the characters' differing temperaments and experiences. Nature in the novel serves as an active participant in the characters’ lives, shaping their identities and their relationships, offering a physical manifestation of their passions, conflicts, and desires.

The moors are a key part of the novel, acting as both a literal and symbolic landscape. The wild, untamed nature of the moors mirrors the chaotic, destructive love between Heathcliff and Catherine. The desolate environment becomes a place where the characters can express their deepest emotions, and where their fates seem to be sealed by the very land they inhabit.

In The Picture of Dorian Gray, deception is examined both in terms of self-deception and the deceiving influence of others. Lord Henry’s manipulation of Dorian serves as the prime example of external deception, as he leads Dorian to believe that beauty and pleasure are the ultimate goals in life. Meanwhile, Dorian deceives himself into believing that his actions have no consequences as long as his outward appearance remains untarnished. The novel critiques the dangers of living in a world of illusion, where the line between reality and deception becomes blurred, ultimately leading to moral decay and destruction.

Dorian’s outward appearance, untouched by the consequences of his actions, is a central symbol of the theme of deception in the novel. His portrait, however, becomes increasingly corrupted, reflecting the true nature of his soul as he continues to deceive both himself and others. This external illusion of innocence contrasts sharply with the inner darkness Dorian experiences.

In Othello, betrayal is explored through the personal and societal dynamics that lead to the destruction of trust. Desdemona’s innocence and loyalty are repeatedly questioned, and the doubt cast upon her purity becomes a form of betrayal in itself. The internalization of betrayal—how it destroys self-worth and sows distrust—plays out not only through Iago’s manipulations but also within Othello’s psyche as he spirals into jealousy and doubt. The play shows how betrayal operates not only through deliberate actions but also through the toxic seeds of suspicion and miscommunication.

Iago’s actions in Othello serve as the primary instrument of betrayal, as he manipulates Othello into believing that Desdemona has been unfaithful. His deceitful actions wreak havoc on the relationships and reputations of the other characters, leading to the ultimate tragedy.

In Moby-Dick, revenge is explored as a consuming force that transcends personal grievances and affects the broader human condition. Ahab's crew, including Ishmael, is drawn into his vengeful pursuit of the whale, yet many of the crew members have their own motivations and desires that become intertwined with Ahab’s quest. Queequeg, for example, despite being a loyal companion to Ishmael, finds his own sense of purpose within the larger voyage, though revenge is not his driving force. The novel critiques the all-encompassing nature of revenge and its ability to distort human relationships and moral values, suggesting that vengeance often comes at the expense of one’s humanity.

Captain Ahab’s obsession with Moby Dick is the central manifestation of revenge in Moby-Dick. Ahab’s single-minded pursuit of the white whale is driven by a personal vendetta, and this consuming desire for revenge ultimately leads to his and the crew’s destruction. His relentless pursuit of the whale becomes a metaphor for the dangers of vengeance.

In Jane Eyre, fate is portrayed as both an external force and a personal challenge that the protagonist must navigate. Jane’s life is shaped by her circumstances, from her early years as an orphan to her struggles as a governess. Despite the constraints imposed by society, her inner strength and sense of self-worth allow her to overcome challenges and carve her own path. The novel suggests that fate is not entirely preordained; instead, Jane's choices, integrity, and resilience determine her future. While external forces may shape her, it is her personal agency that ultimately leads her to a life of fulfillment.

Rochester’s marriage to Bertha, though central to his past, is a hidden obstacle in his relationship with Jane Eyre. The revelation of his marriage and the secret of Bertha’s existence significantly alter the trajectory of their relationship, as Jane must confront the consequences of love and betrayal. This hidden fate serves as a turning point, forcing Jane to make choices that redefine her sense of identity and her understanding of personal integrity.

In Brave New World, freedom is depicted as an illusion, as the society’s rigid control over every aspect of life, from reproduction to recreation, leaves little room for personal choice. While the citizens are given what appears to be freedom—through consumerism and constant pleasure—this freedom is hollow and imposed. The novel critiques the idea that freedom can exist in a society where individuality is suppressed for the sake of stability. Characters like John the Savage embody the tension between personal autonomy and the constraints of a controlled society, highlighting the cost of true freedom in a world that seeks to eliminate discomfort and complexity.

The use of soma in Brave New World is a tool for maintaining control over the population. By dulling emotions and offering an easy escape from discomfort, soma is used to suppress any desire for rebellion or dissatisfaction. The drug keeps individuals from questioning the lack of true freedom in their lives, providing a false sense of happiness and security.

In The Old Man and the Sea, water represents the profound connection between life, struggle, and renewal. It is a symbol of both vitality and challenge, embodying the cycles of persistence and suffering that define the human experience. For Santiago, the water reflects his inner journey, representing a medium through which he confronts his limitations and seeks redemption. The idea of water as a boundary between self and the external world plays out throughout the novel, suggesting that it is not merely a force of nature, but an existential force that shapes one’s fate, pushing individuals toward personal growth and ultimate understanding.

The sea in The Old Man and the Sea plays a vital role in Santiago’s journey, acting both as adversary and companion. Its vast, unpredictable nature embodies the struggles of life that the old man must endure. While Santiago faces the physical challenges of the sea, his encounter is also symbolic of the larger struggle between human will and the uncontrollable forces of nature.

In Frankenstein, alienation is explored through Victor Frankenstein’s obsessive pursuit of knowledge and its consequences. Victor’s isolation from family, friends, and the world around him becomes a self-imposed exile driven by his desire to transcend human limits. His alienation is emotional, intellectual, and moral, as he distances himself from the people who care for him in order to pursue his experiments. This theme reflects the dangers of unchecked ambition and the consequences of isolating oneself from the social and ethical connections that define humanity. The novel suggests that alienation can ultimately lead to destruction, as it isolates the individual not only from others but also from their own humanity.

Frankenstein’s monster’s alienation is central to the novel’s exploration of isolation. Rejected by his creator and the world around him, the monster faces an existence devoid of companionship or understanding, which drives him to seek revenge against Victor Frankenstein.

In Pride and Prejudice, family dynamics shape the characters' social interactions, values, and eventual fates. The Bennet sisters’ relationships with each other—ranging from camaraderie to rivalry—highlight the diverse ways in which family bonds can influence individual behavior and choices. Jane’s gentle nature contrasts with Lydia’s more reckless attitude, reflecting the varying impacts that family expectations and relationships have on the characters. The novel ultimately critiques the societal pressures placed on family life, showing how familial love and loyalty can be both a source of strength and vulnerability.

Elizabeth Bennet’s relationship with her parents is marked by tension, as she often finds herself at odds with their expectations, particularly in regard to marriage. This strained dynamic plays a significant role in shaping Elizabeth’s attitudes toward marriage and social expectations.

In The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, innocence is portrayed as both a personal trait and a lens through which the protagonist views the world. Huck’s journey down the Mississippi River represents a rite of passage, where he encounters a world filled with moral ambiguity and social injustice. Despite these challenges, Huck’s childlike understanding of right and wrong allows him to see things with a clarity that others, more burdened by societal norms, cannot. The novel contrasts Huck’s innocence with the corrupt, hypocritical society around him, showing how innocence can be both a shield and a vulnerability in a world defined by moral contradictions.

Huck’s relationship with Jim plays a pivotal role in the novel, as their friendship challenges the conventions of race and slavery. Through Jim, Huck learns valuable lessons about humanity and morality, which slowly strip away the layers of innocence that society has imposed on him.

In 1984, power is examined as a pervasive force that operates through control of information, language, and history. The Party’s manipulation of truth and its ability to rewrite the past create a reality where individuals are disempowered, unable to trust their own experiences. Power in the novel is not just about physical control, but also about psychological subjugation, as the citizens of Oceania internalize the Party’s ideologies. The novel shows how power operates on multiple levels, influencing behavior, thought, and even reality itself, making resistance seem not only futile but almost impossible.

Big Brother’s control over the citizens of Oceania is central to the theme of power in 1984. As the omnipresent figurehead of the Party, Big Brother represents the totalitarian force that dictates every aspect of life in the society, from surveillance to the manipulation of truth. His image is everywhere, reinforcing the idea that power is not just held by one person but is institutionalized across all aspects of existence.

In Great Expectations, social class is a central theme that influences the characters’ relationships, aspirations, and sense of self-worth. Characters like Joe, who represents the working class, are depicted as grounded in integrity and love, while those with higher social status, like Estella, often express disdain for the lower classes. The novel critiques the rigid class structure, suggesting that social mobility is a double-edged sword. Pip’s experiences with different classes reveal that material wealth and status do not necessarily lead to happiness or fulfillment, but instead often result in isolation and disillusionment.

Pip’s desire to become a gentleman is directly tied to his perception of class and self-worth. His aspiration to rise above his working-class origins shapes much of the narrative, as he believes that wealth and status will provide him with the respect and identity he craves.

In Crime and Punishment, guilt is portrayed not only as a psychological burden but as an existential condition that drives characters to grapple with their sense of self-worth and moral integrity. Sonia’s suffering is tied to the weight of guilt she carries for her past choices, yet her path to redemption contrasts with Raskolnikov’s. The novel suggests that guilt can either paralyze a person or lead them to seek penance and spiritual healing. Through the experiences of various characters, Dostoevsky explores how guilt can be transformative, either pushing one toward despair or opening a door to moral awakening and salvation.

Raskolnikov's reaction to his crime is central to the theme of guilt in Crime and Punishment. His inner turmoil and eventual confession are direct consequences of his overwhelming sense of guilt, which manifests as both a psychological and physical ailment throughout the novel.

In The Scarlet Letter, social expectations are portrayed as powerful forces that shape individuals’ identities and actions. The Puritan society places immense pressure on its members to conform to its strict moral codes, and Hester’s experiences reflect the rigidity of this system. Despite her public shaming, Hester refuses to let society dictate her sense of self-worth, instead choosing to redefine her identity on her own terms. The novel critiques the hypocrisy of a society that demands perfection while failing to uphold its own values, revealing the complexities of social morality and the conflict between individuality and collective norms.

Hester Prynne’s public punishment serves as a harsh reminder of the social expectations in the Puritan community. The scarlet letter she is forced to wear becomes a symbol not only of her sin but of the societal pressure to conform to religious and moral standards, exposing the oppressive nature of the society in which she lives.

In Lord of the Flies, survival is explored through the characters’ efforts to adapt to an isolated, hostile environment. The boys’ initial attempts at creating a structured society—complete with rules and leadership—demonstrate the instinctual need for order and cooperation. However, survival also becomes a matter of resourcefulness, as the boys grapple with the challenges of securing food, shelter, and safety. The novel reflects the tension between human instincts for cooperation and the harshness of nature, showing how the fight for survival can reveal both the noblest and the darkest aspects of human nature.

The boys’ descent into savagery is a direct consequence of their struggle for survival. As their initial efforts to maintain order and civilization break down, they resort to violence and chaos, demonstrating the fragility of social constructs when faced with the primal instincts necessary for survival.

In Macbeth, ambition is portrayed as a consuming force that drives characters to take extreme actions, often leading to moral compromise and personal destruction. Lady Macbeth’s ambition for greatness leads her to manipulate her husband, urging him to commit murder in pursuit of their shared vision. The play critiques how ambition can distort one’s sense of morality, driving individuals to pursue their goals at any cost, even if it means betraying personal values and causing irreversible harm. The destructive power of ambition is reflected in the deterioration of relationships, trust, and mental stability.

Macbeth’s desire for power is the central manifestation of his ambition in the play. His overwhelming need to ascend the throne leads him to commit murder and betray his moral compass, ultimately resulting in his tragic downfall. His ambition becomes his undoing as he is consumed by guilt and paranoia.

In The Crucible, justice is examined through the lens of personal integrity and the collective responsibility of a community. Characters such as John Proctor and Giles Corey strive to uphold their moral beliefs in the face of societal pressure and the chaos of the witch trials. The novel critiques the concept of justice in a society where truth is distorted, and individuals are punished based on unfounded accusations. It shows how the pursuit of justice becomes tainted by fear, superstition, and vengeance, and how the failure to act justly can result in the destruction of both individuals and communities.

The court’s authority in The Crucible becomes a symbol of the miscarriage of justice, as it upholds a system that condemns innocent people based on hysteria and false accusations. The power wielded by the judges and officials ultimately serves to perpetuate the injustice, highlighting the dangers of unchecked authority.

In Frankenstein, isolation is portrayed as a condition that exacerbates suffering and moral deterioration. The monster, abandoned by his creator and rejected by society, experiences profound loneliness, which leads him to seek revenge against his maker. The novel suggests that isolation, whether physical or emotional, can drive individuals to extremes, as it deprives them of meaningful connections and support. Through the experiences of both Victor and the monster, Shelley explores how isolation can undermine the human spirit, leading to despair, rage, and ultimately self-destruction.

Victor Frankenstein’s self-imposed isolation is central to his tragic downfall. His obsession with his scientific pursuits leads him to withdraw from family and society, and this isolation fosters his guilt, despair, and eventual ruin. His failure to connect with others ultimately contributes to the creation of a monster and the ensuing catastrophe.

Austrian School The Austrian School is a heterodox school of economic thought that is based on methodological individualism—the concept that social phenomena result from the motivations and actions of individuals. The Austrian School originated in late-19th and early-20th century Vienna with the work of Carl Menger, Eugen Böhm von Bawerk, Friedrich von Wieser and others. It was methodologically opposed to the Prussian Historical School (in a dispute known as Methodenstreit). Current-day economists working in this tradition are located in many different countries, but their work is still referred to as Austrian economics. Among the theoretical contributions of the early year

of the Austrian School are the subjective theory of value, marginalism in price theory and the formulation of the economic calculation problem, each of which has become an accepted part of mainstream economics. Since the mid-20th century, mainstream economists have been critical of the modern day Austrian School and consider its rejection of mathematical modelling, econometrics and macroeconomic analysis to be outside mainstream economics, or ""heterodox"". Although the Austrian School has been considered heterodox since the late 1930s, it attracted renewed interest in the 1970s after Friedrich Hayek shared the 1974 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences and following the

society that derives its income from legal aggression—an entity that inherently violates the central axiom of libertarianism. Some anarcho-capitalists, such as Rothbard, accept the nonaggression axiom on an intrinsic moral or natural law basis. the waveform. In the frequency domain, amplitude modulation produces a signal with power concentrated at the carrier frequency and two adjacent sidebands. Each sideband is equal in bandwidth to that of the modulating signal, and is a mirror image of the other. Standard AM is thus sometimes called "double-sideband amplitude modulation" (DSB-AM) to distinguish it from more sophisticated modulation methods also based on AM. One disadvantage of all amplitude modulation techniques (not only standard AM) is that the receiver amplifies and detects noise and electromagnetic interference in equal proportion to the signal. According to Statistics Canada, Alberta is home to the second-highest proportion (two percent) of Francophones in western Canada

In The Hunger Games, rebellion is depicted as a response to oppression, where individuals and groups fight back against a corrupt and tyrannical system. Characters like Gale and the citizens of District 12 engage in subtle forms of resistance, challenging the Capitol’s authority in both direct and indirect ways. The novel explores how rebellion arises from the desire for freedom, justice, and self-determination. It shows how small acts of defiance can snowball into larger movements, but also how rebellion often comes with great personal cost, leading to sacrifices, loss, and the questioning of one’s own morals.

Katniss Everdeen’s defiance of the Capitol ignites the spark for rebellion in The Hunger Games. Her actions during the Games serve as a symbol of resistance against the oppressive rule of the Capitol, rallying others to join the fight for freedom. Her role as the "Mockingjay" becomes a focal point for the revolution that spreads throughout the districts.

In Lord of the Flies, human nature is explored through the characters’ varying responses to the isolation they face. The novel suggests that, despite the absence of societal rules, there remains a tension between order and chaos within each individual. Ralph represents the drive for civility and the preservation of structure, while Piggy embodies intellectualism and reason. Their efforts to maintain a sense of civilization are contrasted with others’ inclinations toward individualism and the primal instinct for survival. The novel subtly critiques the fragility of the social contract and how quickly human beings can become consumed by their own desires when they are removed from the structure of society.

The boys’ descent into savagery is a critical exploration of human nature in Lord of the Flies. Their abandonment of civilized behavior leads to the unraveling of their sense of self and morality. The novel demonstrates how isolation from society can expose the darker, more animalistic aspects of human nature, resulting in violence and destruction.

In The Great Gatsby, wealth is portrayed as both a symbol of success and a source of moral decay. Tom Buchanan, for example, uses his family’s wealth to maintain power and control over others, viewing it as a tool for reinforcing his own sense of superiority. Daisy, while seemingly enchanted by the material comforts that wealth affords her, is also trapped by her dependence on it, leading to her inability to break free from the expectations of society. The novel critiques the American Dream by illustrating how wealth does not bring happiness or fulfillment, but rather contributes to the characters’ spiritual emptiness and social entrapment.

Gatsby’s wealth and his extravagant parties serve as the outward manifestation of his desire to win Daisy’s love. His wealth, however, becomes a symbol of his illusory pursuit of the American Dream, ultimately revealing that material success cannot buy genuine happiness or love.

In Romeo and Juliet, love is depicted as a powerful and transformative force that transcends societal boundaries. The intense, passionate connection between the two lovers challenges the expectations of their feuding families and society at large. Their love is youthful and idealistic, representing an escape from the rigid social structures of Verona. It drives them to take bold actions, such as marrying in secret, which shows their willingness to defy tradition for the sake of personal happiness. Shakespeare explores the idea that love, while beautiful and sincere, can also be reckless and impulsive, leading to both moments of joy and the potential for tragedy.

The tragic ending of Romeo and Juliet’s love story underscores the destructive consequences of family conflict and societal pressure. Their love, while genuine, becomes a symbol of the fragility of human existence when placed in the context of external forces that they cannot control.

In The Catcher in the Rye, identity is a central theme as Holden Caulfield struggles to reconcile his sense of self with the expectations of the world around him. His constant searching for meaning reflects the confusion and alienation often experienced by young adults as they transition from adolescence to adulthood. Holden’s disdain for phoniness and his desire to protect the innocence of others, particularly through his fantasy of becoming the "catcher in the rye," reflect his internal conflict with societal norms. His journey is ultimately about discovering who he is in a world that seems overwhelmingly complex and contradictory, highlighting the struggle for individuality in a conformist society.

Holden Caulfield’s relationship with his family plays a significant role in shaping his sense of identity. His interactions with his parents, especially his mother’s absence and his brother’s death, contribute to his feelings of alienation and confusion. The lack of familial support exacerbates his internal conflict, reinforcing his sense of not fitting in.

In Beloved, memory is portrayed as a haunting force that shapes the lives of the characters, even when the past seems to be buried. The novel demonstrates how individuals, particularly those who have endured trauma, often live in a tension between remembering and forgetting. The community’s collective memory of slavery plays a role in how they relate to Sethe and the other survivors, revealing the deep scars left by historical and personal experiences. Memory is not just a personal experience but a communal one, influencing the way individuals understand their identity and their place in a larger, often painful, history.

Sethe’s traumatic memories of her past are central to the novel’s exploration of memory and its impact on the present. These memories, particularly her experiences as a slave, resurface in the form of haunting visions and emotional scars, dictating her actions and relationships as she struggles to reconcile her past with her future.

In Frankenstein, knowledge is explored as both a powerful tool for advancement and a dangerous force that can lead to destruction. The characters’ pursuit of knowledge is often motivated by a desire to understand the mysteries of life, yet this pursuit is presented as flawed when it disregards ethical concerns. Characters like Walton seek knowledge for intellectual and personal growth, but they are warned by Victor’s tale of how unchecked curiosity can lead to irreversible consequences. The novel critiques the Enlightenment-era belief in the power of reason and knowledge, suggesting that wisdom must accompany knowledge to prevent it from becoming a destructive force.

Victor Frankenstein’s obsession with scientific discovery drives him to push the boundaries of knowledge. His relentless pursuit of understanding the secrets of life and death leads him to create the monster, a decision that ultimately brings ruin to both him and those around him.

In The Odyssey, loyalty is a key theme that drives the actions of several characters, both mortal and divine. The loyalty of Odysseus’ crew is evident as they follow their leader despite the overwhelming challenges they face during their journey home. Similarly, the loyalty of Telemachus to his father demonstrates the importance of family bonds, as he searches for news of Odysseus and defends his home from the suitors. Even the gods show loyalty to Odysseus, offering him assistance when needed, reflecting the idea that loyalty is reciprocated in both human and divine relationships. The novel emphasizes that loyalty, whether to family, friends, or gods, is an essential element for survival and success.

Odysseus' relationship with Penelope is one of the strongest examples of loyalty in The Odyssey. Penelope remains faithful to her husband throughout his long absence, waiting for his return despite the pressure from suitors. Her loyalty symbolizes the steadfastness of the home and family, while Odysseus' own loyalty to her propels his determination to return.

In The Handmaid’s Tale, freedom is a central theme that is explored through the lens of the oppressive totalitarian regime of Gilead. The novel illustrates how freedom is stripped away from individuals, particularly women, and how the structures of power limit personal agency. While the society at large is rigidly controlled, the novel also portrays the ways in which characters attempt to find subtle forms of freedom, whether through small acts of defiance or through the imagination. The theme of freedom is also juxtaposed with the illusion of safety, as the characters navigate a world that presents the cost of any semblance of autonomy. At its core, The Handmaid’s Tale highlights the fragility of freedom in the face of overwhelming societal control.

Offred’s personal rebellion against the regime is one of the few ways in which she tries to reclaim her autonomy. However, her efforts are cautious, knowing that any act of defiance could result in severe punishment. Her rebellion speaks to the broader theme of the loss of freedom in a society that demands conformity and obedience.

In The Brothers Karamazov, wisdom is portrayed as a multifaceted concept that transcends intellectual knowledge. Alyosha Karamazov embodies a kind of spiritual wisdom that is grounded in love, humility, and faith. His quiet strength contrasts with the more overt, rational wisdom represented by other characters, showing how true wisdom is often expressed through compassion and understanding. The novel also suggests that wisdom is not only about answering the great questions of life, but also about accepting the complexity of the human condition and living with integrity. Through Alyosha’s journey, Dostoevsky reveals that wisdom can come from faith, experience, and the willingness to embrace the uncertainties of life.

Ivan Karamazov’s philosophical arguments, particularly regarding the existence of God and the problem of suffering, provide a stark contrast to Alyosha’s spiritual wisdom. Ivan represents a more intellectual form of wisdom, grounded in logic and reason, yet his inability to reconcile his beliefs leads him to a profound existential crisis.

Diagnosis of a skin abscess is usually made based on what it looks like and is confirmed by cutting it open. Ultrasound imaging may be useful in cases in which the diagnosis is not clear. In abscesses around the anus, computer tomography (CT) may be important to look for deeper infection. Standard treatment for most skin or soft tissue abscesses is cutting it open and drainage. There appears to be some benefit from also using antibiotics. A small amount of evidence supports not packing the cavityWW

Abscess An abscess is a collection of pus that has built up within the tissue of the body. Signs and symptoms of abscesses include redness, pain, warmth, and swelling. The swelling may feel fluid-filled when pressed. The area of redness often extends beyond the swelling. Carbuncles and boils are types of abscess that often involve hair follicles, with carbuncles being larger. They are usually caused by a bacterial infection. Often many different types of bacteria are involved in a single infection. In the United States and many other areas of the world the most common bacteria present is ""methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus

Afghanistan's economy is the world's 108th largest, with a GDP of $64.08 billion; the country fares much worse in terms of per-capita GDP (PPP), ranking 167th out of 186 countries in a 2016 report from the International Monetary Fund. Rome's enemies in Germania. A prime example of Roman loss in battle was the Battle of Teutoburg Forest in AD 9, where three entire legions led by Publius Quinctilius Varus were destroyed by Arminius, leader of the Cherusci, an apparent Roman ally. Augustus retaliated by dispatching Tiberius and Drusus to the Rhineland to pacify it, which had some success although the battle of AD 9 brought the end to Roman expansion into Germany. Between 1991 (after the enactment of the ADA) and 1995, the employment rate of men with disabilities dropped by 7.8% regardless of age, educational level, or type of disability, with the most affected being young, less-educated and mentally disabled men. In Dracula, the supernatural serves as a symbol for the unknown and the fears of the Victorian era. While the vampire myth is at the heart of the story, the supernatural elements are used to explore themes of control, invasion, and the boundaries between the natural and unnatural. The eerie presence of the undead, as well as other supernatural occurrences like blood-drinking and hypnotism, reflect the anxiety surrounding the loss of control and the corruption of purity. Throughout the novel, the supernatural is both a literal force and a metaphor for the darker impulses of humanity—fear, lust, and power. The theme of the supernatural is also tied to the power of knowledge, as the characters attempt to combat Dracula's forces using scientific and spiritual means.

Dracula’s ability to transform into different creatures plays a central role in his supernatural power. His transformations allow him to manipulate others and become a nearly unstoppable force. This ability is a key part of his terrifying influence over the characters and adds to the novel's exploration of power and domination.

In The Stranger, isolation is explored on an existential level, primarily through the lens of Meursault’s disconnection from society and the people around him. His inability to conform to social norms and express the emotions expected of him creates a profound sense of isolation. This disconnection is evident in his relationships, or lack thereof, as he remains emotionally distant from his mother, his girlfriend Marie, and even his own sense of guilt regarding the murder he commits. The novel suggests that isolation is not merely a physical condition but also a psychological state, where the individual is estranged from the societal and emotional connections that define human existence.

Meursault’s physical isolation in prison is a culmination of his emotional and existential alienation. The stark loneliness he faces in this confined space amplifies his internal disconnection, as he continues to reject the expectations of the world around him, leading to a deeper sense of detachment.

In Wuthering Heights, fate operates through the intertwined lives of the characters, where past events continue to haunt the present. The novel suggests that certain actions and emotions are predestined, with characters seemingly unable to escape the destructive patterns of their ancestors. The presence of spirits, dreams, and supernatural occurrences symbolizes the inescapable pull of fate, while the generational cycles of revenge and bitterness echo the idea that certain fates are inevitable. The tension between free will and fate is a key theme, as characters wrestle with their actions while being pulled toward outcomes beyond their control.

The deaths of Catherine and Heathcliff highlight the tragic culmination of their doomed love and the forces of fate that bind them. Their deaths are symbolic of the destructive power of love and revenge, and the inevitability of tragedy that defines the novel’s exploration of fate.

In Animal Farm, power is explored as both a corrupting and sustaining force, particularly in how it is wielded to control and shape society. The animals’ initial hopes for a more just and equal society are subverted by the gradual rise of authoritarianism. The struggle for power is not just a political theme, but one that pervades the everyday lives of the animals, as leaders rise and fall, and the societal structure becomes more rigid. The theme of power is also reflected in the way language is used to manipulate thought and control perception, as slogans and commandments are twisted to suit the needs of those in power. Orwell critiques how power, even when gained with noble intentions, can lead to tyranny and exploitation.

Napoleon’s manipulation of the animals is a clear illustration of how power corrupts and is used for personal gain. His control over the farm symbolizes the authoritarian power that emerges from the ideals of revolution, showing how those in power can exploit and oppress those who once fought for equality.

In Les Misérables, sacrifice is a central theme that drives many of the characters to pursue acts of heroism and moral redemption. Fantine’s sacrifice of her dignity and well-being for the sake of her daughter Cosette reflects the selflessness that defines maternal love. Similarly, Marius’ involvement in the student revolution represents a willingness to sacrifice his life for the ideals of justice and freedom. The novel illustrates how sacrifice is often tied to personal suffering, yet it also holds the potential for creating change. Sacrifice in Les Misérables becomes a transformative act, shaping the lives of others and impacting the course of history.

Jean Valjean’s acts of self-sacrifice are central to his moral journey in Les Misérables. His decision to give up his own freedom to protect others, especially Cosette, exemplifies his transformation from a hardened criminal to a man of compassion and honor.

In 1984, technology serves as a tool not just for surveillance but also for controlling the flow of information and shaping reality. The Party manipulates technology to create an environment where truth is malleable and history is constantly rewritten. Through the use of machines like the telescreen and the controlled distribution of knowledge, technology becomes a means to stifle independent thought and enforce ideological conformity. The novel underscores how technological advancements can be repurposed by totalitarian regimes to consolidate power, illustrating the dangers of technological progress when it is used to control rather than liberate.

The Party’s surveillance system, through devices like the telescreen, is one of the most evident ways technology is employed to monitor and control citizens in 1984. The constant surveillance ensures that no thoughts or actions remain private, stripping individuals of their autonomy.

In Moby-Dick, memory plays a crucial role in shaping the identities of various characters and their relationships to the world around them. Ishmael’s reflections throughout the novel are laced with memories of past experiences, both personal and collective. These memories, whether of previous voyages or philosophical musings, shape his perception of the present and of the monstrous whale. Memory also appears in the form of cultural stories, legends, and myths about the sea, which inform the sailors' understanding of their purpose. In this way, memory serves as a lens through which the characters navigate their own internal and external struggles, emphasizing the importance of recollection in forming one’s worldview.

Captain Ahab’s obsessive memories of the white whale and his lost leg drive his singular pursuit of vengeance. His fixation on these memories distorts his sense of reality, leading him to sacrifice everything for his personal vendetta against the whale.

In The Catcher in the Rye, innocence is portrayed as both a precious and fleeting state, with Holden Caulfield grappling with the loss of it throughout the novel. His idealization of children, especially his fantasy of being the "catcher in the rye," reflects his desire to preserve the innocence of youth and protect others from the harshness of the adult world. He yearns for a world where children remain unspoiled by the phoniness he perceives in adults, indicating his struggle to reconcile his own disillusionment with the purity he associates with childhood. The novel portrays the fragility of innocence and the inevitable transition into adulthood, marked by loss and disillusionment.

Holden's thoughts on his younger brother Allie represent a deep sense of loss of innocence. Allie's untimely death forces Holden to confront the unfairness of life and the pain of growing up, as he desperately clings to memories of his brother's purity.

In Crime and Punishment, guilt manifests in various forms, beyond the immediate remorse felt by Raskolnikov. Sonia’s sense of guilt, stemming from her circumstances as a prostitute, contrasts with Raskolnikov’s more abstract moral quandaries. Her self-sacrifice and suffering serve as a reflection of the weight of guilt and the path to redemption. Similarly, other characters, such as Dunya, experience guilt as part of their personal sacrifices for the sake of family. Dostoevsky explores how guilt can shape actions and relationships, suggesting that it can either lead to self-destruction or, conversely, to personal growth and spiritual awakening, depending on how one responds to it.

Raskolnikov’s internal struggles after the murder are central to the theme of guilt in Crime and Punishment. His growing sense of guilt becomes unbearable, leading him to confess his crime and ultimately seek redemption.

In The Great Gatsby, memory is not just a personal experience but also a societal construct, shaped by the expectations and perceptions of the era. The characters are haunted by their recollections of the past, yet the narrative itself is steeped in selective memory. Gatsby’s grandiose reinvention of himself is an attempt to erase the past and create a new identity, while Nick Carraway’s own reflections on the events of the summer serve as a commentary on how memory distorts the reality of the past. Fitzgerald illustrates how the past is often romanticized or misremembered, emphasizing the tension between personal history and the illusions that people cling to in order to justify their present.

Gatsby’s recollections of his past with Daisy are pivotal to the narrative of The Great Gatsby. His idealized memories drive his relentless pursuit of recreating their former love, blinding him to the reality of their present situation.

Allegory As a literary device, an allegory is a metaphor in which a character, place or event is used to deliver a broader message about real-world issues and occurrences. Allegory (in the sense of the practice and use of allegorical devices and works) has occurred widely throughout history in all forms of art, largely because it can readily illustrate or convey complex ideas and concepts in ways that are comprehensible or striking to its viewers, readers, or listeners. Writers or speakers typically use allegories as literary devices or as rhetorical devices that convey (semi-)hidden or complex meanings through symbolic figures,

According to legend, Castor and Pollux announced to one of their ancestors the victory of the Romans over the Latins at the battle of Lake Regillus, and, to confirm the truth of what they had just said, they stroked his black hair and beard, which immediately became red. Artist An artist is a person engaged in an activity related to creating art, practicing the arts, or demonstrating an art. Actium Actium or Aktion () was a town on a promontory in ancient Acarnania at the entrance of the Ambraciot Gulf off which Augustus gained his celebrated victory over Antony and Cleopatra, on September 2, 31 BCE. Actium belonged originally to the Corinthian colonists of Anactorium, who probably founded the sanctuary of Apollo Actius. This temple was of great antiquity. In the 3rd century BCE it fell to the Acarnanians, who subsequently held their religious summits there. There was also an ancient festival named Actia, celebrated here in honour of the god. The anthropology of religion involves the study of religious institutions in relation to other social institutions, and the comparison of religious beliefs and practices across cultures. Modern anthropology assumes that there is complete continuity between magical thinking and religion, and that every religion is a cultural product, created by the human community that worships it. Urban anthropology is concerned with issues of urbanization, poverty, and neoliberalism. Ulf Hannerz quotes a 1960s remark that traditional anthropologists were "a notoriously agoraphobic lot, anti-urban by definition".

Al-Qaeda operates as a network of Islamic extremists and Salafist jihadists. Alexander Anderson (mathematician) Alexander Anderson ( in Aberdeen – in Paris) was a Scottish mathematician. He was born in Aberdeen, possibly in 1582, according to a print which suggests he was aged 35 in 1617. It is unknown where he was educated, but it is likely that he initially studied writing and philosophy (the "belles lettres") in his home city of Aberdeen. He then went to the continent, and was a professor of mathematics in Paris by the start of the seventeenth century. There he published or edited, between the years 1612 and 1619, various geometric and algebraic tracts. He

Anthemius of Tralles Anthemius of Tralles (, Medieval Greek: , "Anthémios o Trallianós"; – 533 558) was a Greek from Tralles who worked as a geometer and architect in Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire. With Isidore of Miletus, he designed the Hagia Sophia for Justinian I. Anthemius was one of the five sons of Stephanus of Tralles, a physician. His brothers were Dioscorus, Alexander, Olympius, and Metrodorus. Assembly line An assembly line is a manufacturing process (often called a "progressive assembly") in which parts (usually interchangeable parts) are added as the semi-finished assembly moves from workstation to workstation where the parts are added in sequence until the final assembly is produced. By mechanically moving the parts to the assembly work and moving the semi-finished assembly from work station to work station, a finished product can be assembled faster and with less labor than by having workers carry parts to a stationary piece for assembly. Assembly lines are common methods of assembling complex items such as automobiles and

The problem of drug resistance in malaria has driven efforts to develop vaccines. Trypanosomes are parasitic protozoa that cause African trypanosomiasis and Chagas disease (American trypanosomiasis). There are no vaccines to prevent these infections so drugs such as pentamidine and suramin, benznidazole and nifurtimox are used to treat infections. These drugs are effective but infections caused by resistant parasites have been reported. Leishmaniasis is caused by protozoa and is an important public health problem worldwide, especially in sub-tropical and tropical countries. Drug resistance has "become a major concern". Artistic revolution Throughout history, forms of art have gone through periodic abrupt changes called artistic revolutions. Movements have come to an end to be replaced by a new movement markedly different in striking ways. See also cultural movements. The role of fine art has been to simultaneously express values of the current culture while also offering criticism, balance, or alternatives to any such values that are proving no longer useful. So as times change, art changes. If changes were abrupt they were deemed revolutions. The best artists have predated society's changes due not to any prescience, but because sensitive perceptivity

Astatine Astatine is a radioactive chemical element with symbol At and atomic number 85. It is the rarest naturally occurring element in the Earth's crust, occurring only as the decay product of various heavier elements. All of astatine's isotopes are short-lived; the most stable is astatine-210, with a half-life of 8.1 hours. A sample of the pure element has never been assembled, because any macroscopic specimen would be immediately vaporized by the heat of its own radioactivity. The bulk properties of astatine are not known with any certainty. Many of them have been estimated based on the element's position on

Christian anarchism is a [[Christian movement|movement]] in [[political theology]] that combines anarchism and Christianity. Its main proponents included [[Leo Tolstoy]], Dorothy Day, Ammon Hennacy and [[Jacques Ellul]]. [[Religious anarchism]] refers to a set of related anarchist ideologies that are inspired by the teachings of (organized) religions, but many anarchists have traditionally been skeptical of and opposed to [[organized religion]]. Many different religions have served as inspiration for religious forms of anarchism, most notably Christianity as Christian anarchists believe that biblical teachings give credence to anarchist philosophy. Non-Christian forms of religious anarchism include [[Buddhist anarchism]], [[Jewish anarchism]] and most recently [[Neopaganism]].

Antibiotic resistance increases with duration of treatment. Therefore, as long as an effective minimum is kept, shorter courses of antibiotics are likely to decrease rates of resistance, reduce cost, and have better outcomes with fewer complications. Short course regimens exist for community-acquired pneumonia spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, suspected lung infections in intense care wards, so-called acute abdomen, middle ear infections, sinusitis and throat infections, and penetrating gut injuries. In some situations a short course may not cure the infection as well as a long course. A BMJ editorial recommended that antibiotics can often be safely stopped 72 hours after symptoms resolve.

Ammonius Hermiae Ammonius Hermiae (; "Ammōnios ho Hermeiou"; ) was a Greek philosopher, and the son of the Neoplatonist philosophers Hermias and Aedesia. He was a pupil of Proclus in Athens, and taught at Alexandria for most of his life, writing commentaries on Plato, Aristotle, and other philosophers. Ammonius' father, Hermias, died when he was a child, and his mother, Aedesia, raised him and his brother, Heliodorus, in Alexandria. When they reached adulthood, Aedesia accompanied her sons to Athens where they studied under Proclus. Eventually, they returned to Alexandria, where Ammonius, as head of the Neoplatonist school in Alexandria, lectured

Alexander Aetolus Alexander Aetolus (, "Ἀléxandros ὁ Aἰtōlós") was a Greek poet and grammarian, the only known representative of Aetolian poetry. Alexander was the son of Satyrus and Stratocleia, and was a native of Pleuron in Aetolia, although he spent the greater part of his life at Alexandria, where he was reckoned one of the seven tragic poets who constituted the Tragic Pleiad. Alexander flourished about 280 BC, in the reign of Ptolemy II Philadelphus. He had an office in the Library of Alexandria, and was commissioned by Ptolemy to make a collection of all the tragedies and satyric dramas

International Atomic Time International Atomic Time (TAI, from the French name ) is a high-precision atomic coordinate time standard based on the notional passage of proper time on Earth's geoid. It is the principal realisation of Terrestrial Time (with a fixed offset of epoch). It is also the basis for Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), which is used for civil timekeeping all over the Earth's surface. , when another leap second was added, TAI is exactly 37 seconds ahead of UTC. Anxiety Anxiety is an emotion characterized by an unpleasant state of inner turmoil, often accompanied by nervous behaviour such as pacing back and forth, somatic complaints, and rumination. It is the subjectively unpleasant feelings of dread over anticipated events, such as the feeling of imminent death. Anxiety is not the same as fear, which is a response to a real or perceived immediate threat, whereas anxiety involves the expectation of future threat. Anxiety is a feeling of uneasiness and worry, usually generalized and unfocused as an overreaction to a situation that is only subjectively seen as menacing. It is often

DNA stained with crystal violet can be viewed under natural light without the use of a UV transilluminator which is an advantage, however it may not produce a strong band. Alternative medicine Alternative medicine, fringe medicine, pseudomedicine or simply questionable medicine is promotion or use of practices which are unproven, disproven, impossible to prove, or excessively harmful in relation to their effect — in the attempt to achieve the healing effects of medicine. It differs from experimental medicine in that the latter employs responsible investigation, and is discarded when shown ineffective. The scientific consensus is that alternative therapies either do not, or cannot, work. In some cases laws of nature are violated by their basic claims; in others the treatment is so much worse that its use is unethical. Alternative

Andriscus Andriscus (, "Andrískos"), also often referenced as Pseudo-Philip, was the last King of Macedon ( 149–148 BC). A pretender, who claimed to be the son of Perseus of Macedon, he was a fuller from Adramyttium in Aeolis in western Anatolia. His reign lasted just one year. In 168 BC, the Romans invaded Macedonia and overthrew king Perseus in the First Battle of Pydna. Anglicanism places on the sacraments as a means of grace, sanctification and salvation as expressed in the church's liturgy and doctrine. Ardipithecus Ardipithecus is a genus of an extinct hominine that lived during Late Miocene and Early Pliocene in Afar Depression, Ethiopia. Originally described as one of the earliest ancestors of humans after they diverged from the chimpanzees, the relation of this genus to human ancestors and whether it is a hominin is now a matter of debate. Two fossil species are described in the literature: "A. ramidus", which lived about 4.4 million years ago during the early Pliocene, and "A. kadabba", dated to approximately 5.6 million years ago (late Miocene).
